title: Touch sensor: use your body as input
learnerProfile: adult beginner; curious and capable; no electronics assumed
mission: Wire an LED to GPIO 21, leave one bare jumper on GPIO 14 as a touch pad, and upload the sketch that flips the lamp every time your fingertip taps the wire. The pad has no button and no moving part — the pin can feel the tiny capacitance your body adds when you touch the bare metal. Read your own touched and untouched numbers, and you'll see exactly where to draw the line that means a touch.

sourceMetadata.imageAlt: Official Freenove circuit for the touch lamp: a bare jumper wire on GPIO 14 acting as a touch pad, and a LED on GPIO 21 through a 220 ohm resistor to ground, shown as both a schematic and a breadboard photo.
sourceMetadata.licenseNote: Based on Freenove official material released under CC BY-NC-SA 3.0; preserve attribution and non-affiliation language.

parts.name: ESP32-S3 board
parts.explanation: A development board is a friendly package around a tiny computer chip. The ESP32-S3 runs your uploaded sketch, controls pins, reads sensors, and can also use USB, Wi-Fi, and Bluetooth. In this lesson, it sends the ping signal and measures the echo time.

parts.name: GPIO extension board
parts.explanation: GPIO means general-purpose input/output. These pins are connection points your code can use. The extension board makes them easier to see, reach, and wire without cramming everything onto the ESP32-S3 itself.

parts.name: LED
parts.explanation: A LED is a one-way light. It only lights when its longer leg (the +) faces the pin and its shorter leg (the −) faces ground. Too much current burns it out, so a LED always sits behind a resistor.

parts.name: 220 Ω resistor
parts.explanation: A resistor limits how much current flows. Placed in series with the LED, the 220 Ω one keeps the current gentle enough to light the LED instead of burning it out. The bands of colour print its value, and it has no direction.

parts.name: 3 × jumper wires
parts.explanation: Jumper wires make temporary connections without soldering. Female-to-male wires have a socket on one end and a pin on the other, useful when a sensor module and board use different connector styles.

parts.name: Arduino IDE
parts.explanation: Arduino IDE is the desktop app that opens the sketch, compiles it, uploads it to the ESP32-S3, and shows messages from the board in Serial Monitor.

wiring.reason: The exposed metal tip is the touch pad your fingertip charges.
This pin holds the lamp on or off between touches.
Completes the LED's path back to zero volts.

coachInstructions: Teach the mechanism plainly- the pin charges the bare wire and measures the charge it holds, a fingertip adds capacitance so the number climbs, and no button or moving part is involved.

A threshold is any line drawn between the resting reading and the touched reading; the sketch uses a high one (PRESS_VAL) and a lower one (RELEASE_VAL) so a wavering reading can't flip the lamp back and forth.
Reassure the learner there is no sensor module to find — the touch pad is a single bare-ended jumper on GPIO 14.
Keep the two pins straight — T14 is GPIO 14 for the touch pad and the LED lives on GPIO 21; never let them swap in conversation or wiring.
On this board a touch RAISES the touchRead value (idle in the tens of thousands, touch above 200000); correct any advice claiming readings near zero mean touch.

When tuning, have the learner print their own idle and touched numbers with Sketch_09.1_TouchRead and pick PRESS_VAL and RELEASE_VAL from the gap they see, rather than trusting the stock values blindly.
If the LED flickers, coach them to hold the jumper by its insulation and touch only the bare metal tip, deliberately.
If the Serial Monitor shows nothing, check 115200 baud, the board, and the port before touching code.
steps: Seat the ESP32-S3 on the GPIO extension board and keep USB unplugged while you wire.
Place the LED so its long leg (+) is on the GPIO 21 side and its short leg (−) heads toward ground.
Put the 220 Ω resistor in series between GPIO 21 and the LED's long leg.
Push one end of a jumper into GPIO 14 and leave its other end free in the air — that bare metal tip is your touch pad.
Compare every wire to the chart, then plug in USB.
Open Sketch_09.1_TouchRead.ino first, upload it, and open Serial Monitor at 115200 — note the reading when the wire is alone and when you pinch its bare tip.
Tap the bare tip — the LED flips on. Tap again — it flips off.

arduino.explanation: Measures the charge on touch pin T14, which is GPIO 14 — your fingertip drives this number far higher.
On this board a reading above 200000 counts as a touch; a reading below 60000 counts as a release.
Remembers that the current touch has already been handled, so one tap flips the lamp exactly once.
Reads the LED pin's current state and writes the opposite — the flip itself.

theoryModel.plainLanguage: The pin charges the bare wire and measures the charge it holds; a fingertip adds capacitance, the number climbs, and you compare it to a threshold you pick to decide a touch.
theoryModel.formula: your finger's capacitance lifts the reading past 200000 (press); releasing lets it fall below 60000 (release)

notes.title: Why no button and no moving part
notes.body: Nothing presses or closes here. The pin senses the change in its own electric field as your finger comes near, so a bare wire — even one behind a panel or a sheet of glass — does the whole job. This is the same sensing behind phone screens and elevator buttons.

notes.title: Why the threshold sits between resting and touched
notes.body: The resting reading and the touched reading are two separated levels, and a threshold is any line you draw between them — above it means touched, below it means clear. The sketch draws two lines, a high one (PRESS_VAL) to call a press and a lower one (RELEASE_VAL) to call release, so a reading wavering near a single line can't flip the lamp back and forth.

notes.title: On this board a touch pushes the number up
notes.body: Idle, the pin reads in the tens of thousands; a firm touch climbs past 200000. Older ESP32 chips reported touch as a drop toward zero, and some reference text still describes that — on your S3, trust the thresholds in the sketch and the numbers you read yourself.

expectedOutputExample: Tap the bare end of the wire — the LED flips on and stays on.
Tap it again — the LED flips off.
Serial Monitor at 115200 echoes each touch with "Touch detected! " and each let-go with "Released! ".
test.successCriteria: This lamp latches — the LED holds its state between taps, with your fingertip doing the whole job of a button.

firstChecks: The thresholds may sit wrong for your setup — upload Sketch_09.1_TouchRead and read your own touched and untouched numbers.
Hold the wire by its insulation and touch only the bare metal end, deliberately.
Set the monitor to 115200 baud, then re-check the board and port selections.
Swap in a data-capable USB cable.

challenge: Print your own idle and touched numbers with Sketch_09.1_TouchRead, pick PRESS_VAL and RELEASE_VAL from the gap you actually see, then tune the lamp to answer only a firm grip.
